topic: A person finds out they were adopted through a DNA test
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sterile white of the lab report seemed to mock the crumpled paper in my hand. Every line of the genetic breakdown was a cruel dissection of a life I never knew. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that did little to fill the sudden hollowness in my chest. I traced the unfamiliar names on the document, the letters swimming before my eyes, each one a shard of glass digging deeper. When I finally looked up, the familiar living room felt alien, the comfortable silence suddenly deafening. I stood by the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, feeling a profound, heavy stillness settle over me, as if the very air had turned to lead.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the numbers blurring into meaningless symbols. The confirmation was undeniable, a stark, cold truth laid bare before me. A tremor ran through my hands, making the phone slip slightly in my grasp. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to physically contain the rising tide of distress that threatened to pull me under. When I opened them, the cheerful colours of the room seemed garish, overwhelming. I walked to the kitchen, gripping the edge of the counter until my knuckles were white, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs. A low, ragged sound escaped my throat, more of a sob than a word, and I quickly wiped at my eyes, desperate to hide the sheer weight of the revelation.

[story 3]

The DNA results sat on the desk like an accusation. I picked up the pen, but the ink refused to flow, sticking to the paper. A strange numbness had taken hold, a thick, insulating layer that kept the sharp edges of the news from piercing through, yet underneath it, a deep ache resonated. I paced the length of the living room, my movements jerky and undirected. Each step seemed to pull me further away from the reality I had just uncovered. When I finally stopped by the doorway, I leaned against the frame, closing my eyes tightly. A single tear escaped, tracing a hot, slow path down my cheek, and I felt utterly depleted, as if the energy to move, to even breathe properly, had been completely drained away.
